dragCellAnswer: Drag is a horizontal, inline (axial) force, so the correct sensor is an S-TYPE (S-beam) load cell, not a bar/single-point cell. Mount it inline along the airflow on rod-end (heim) bearings so it sees pure axial load. For a ~10 N drag, the deck's 200 kg S-cell is grossly oversized (~0.5% of range = poor resolution); a ~2 kg S-beam is optimal. Buy the Phidgets 2 kg S-Type (FRC4160_0, ~$40) or 10 kg (FRC4161_0, $40) at phidgets.com; FUTEK LSB200 / Omega LC101 are premium lab-grade alternatives.
summary: The deck's wind-tunnel load-cell content is largely accurate and well-sourced: cell-type selection (bar cells as cantilevers for lift/pitch, S-beam inline for drag), HX711 wiring, RATE-pin behavior, the horizontal-compliance decoupling principle, external-balance load path, and full-rig calibration with a cross-axis matrix all check out. Two items needed correction: the 200 kg drag-cell capacity is far too large for a few-newton drag (use ~2 kg), and the pitching-moment formula conflated two reference points. Wire-color coding should also be flagged as a common convention, not a guaranteed standard.
corrections: 200 kg drag cell is ~20-40x oversized for a few-N drag - use a ~2 kg (5-10 kg OK) S-beam.
Pitch: name the reference point - about one cell = F_other x d; about the midpoint = (front-rear) x d/2.
Wire colors red/black/green/white are a common convention, not universal - verify per datasheet/meter.
Load path is right, but pair it with a sting tare run (subtract support drag measured with no model).

summaryShort: Fact-checked against the sources below: the deck's wind-tunnel load-cell content is largely accurate and well-sourced (cell-type choice, HX711 wiring, RATE pin, compliance decoupling, external-balance load path, full-rig + cross-axis calibration all check out). Four items were flagged:
